title: Two-sheet vat lamination
subtitle: Couching a second wet sheet onto a first to bond them into a single thick sheet
excerpt: Vat lamination, couching one wet sheet directly on top of another before pressing, produces a double-thick paper without glue. The two layers bond as the fibres interlock during pressing and drying. Used to make heavyweight sheets for bookbinding endpapers, pasteboard for covers, and decorative papers where two surfaces (different colours, textures, or embedded materials) should appear on opposite faces.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943), Hunter describes lamination as a traditional technique for making pasteboard and heavy cover papers. The technique appears in European papermaking records continuously from the fifteenth century.

glossaryTerms.definition: The action of pressing a freshly formed wet sheet face-down onto a damp cloth (the couch) to release it from the mould. The wet fibres bond to the cloth surface and the mould can be lifted away.
A stack of alternating couched sheets and cloths built up in sequence for pressing. In traditional papermaking, an entire day's production was assembled into a post before pressing in a screw press.
Placing a wet sheet directly onto another wet sheet, without an intervening cloth, so the fibres of both sheets can bond during pressing and drying.

content.text: one wet sheet onto another works because paper fibres bond to each other when pressed together in a wet state, the same mechanism that makes a freshly formed sheet hold together at all. The technique requires the two sheets to be at the same stage of wetness: both must come straight from the vat, with no drying.

items.name: Pulp in the vat, two batches (or two colours for a two-tone lamination)
Couching cloths
items.qty: at least 3, one for base, one for cover, one for the post

content.text: Pull the first sheet in the normal way and couch it onto a damp cloth on a press board.
Pull a second sheet immediately, without allowing the first to dry. Do not place a cloth between the first and second sheet,
the two wet sheets directly. Couch the second sheet face-down directly onto the first, aligning the edges as closely as possible.

Use the sponge to blot the mould before lifting it away, so the second sheet stays on the first rather than lifting with the mould. The two wet sheets should now be in contact, bonded by the weight of water.
under pressure with a press board and heavy weights. Leave for at least 30 minutes. Dry flat or hung; the two layers will be inseparable once dry.

Pull the first sheet from one colour of pulp and the second from another. Once bonded and dried, the laminated sheet has different colours on its two faces. This is the technique for bookbinding endpapers that are one colour on the paste-down and another on the free end.

items.symptom: The two sheets delaminate when dry
items.cause: Too much time elapsed between forming the first and second sheets; the first sheet had already begun to dry.
items.fix: Form and couch both sheets in quick succession. If working alone, have the second batch ready before pulling the first.

items.symptom: The sheets bond but with a visible seam
items.cause: The sheets were not pressed firmly enough, or the pulp fibres are too long to interlock cleanly.
items.fix: Increase the pressing weight. For short-fibre pulps (cotton linter, recycled office paper) this is rarely a problem; it is more common with long-fibre pulps such as nettle or flax.
